content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


May 6, 1992


Honorable Susan Mullen
City Councilmember
City of Morro Bay
595 Harbor Street
Morro Bay, CA 93442

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-243

Dear Councilmember Mullen:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice with respect to your responsibilities as a Morro Bay City Councilmember pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTION

May you participate in decisions of the city council regarding garbage collection where the contractor that performs garbage collection services has also bid on a separate recycling contract with the city, the same contract for which the Environmental Center, a source of income to you, has bid?

CONCLUSION

So long as the decisions on garbage collection will have no financial effect on the recycling contract or the Environmental Center, you may participate in the decisions regarding garbage collection service.

FACTS

The City of Morro Bay has been considering various issues with respect to the city's garbage and recycling contract. In February 1992, the city decided to deal with the garbage and

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-92-243
Page 2


recycling contracts separately. The garbage contract has been
awarded to a local garbage service. The recycling contract is
currently out for bid. Until a recycling contract is awarded by
the city, the new garbage contractor is also performing recycling
services.

You were formerly employed with the Environmental Center (the
"Center") and presumably received more than $250 from the Center
over the past 12 months. The Center operates a recycling center
and has submitted a bid to handle the city's recycling. In
addition, the garbage contractor has also bid for the new
recycling contract. You have asked whether you have a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions regarding garbage collection in
the city.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest, Generally

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency. This definition would include a
member of the Morro Bay City Council.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

You stated that you were an employee of the Environmental
Center until November 1991. The Environmental Center operates a
nonprofit recycling center in your jurisdiction. We presume that
the amount of income you received from the Environmental Center
during the 12 months prior to the decision in question was greater
than $250. Thus, the Environmental Center would be a source of
income to you, and you are prohibited from participating in any

Our File No. A-92-243
Page 3


decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable² material
financial effect on the Environmental Center.

The Commission has adopted different regulations for the
determination of materiality. The applicable standard depends on
the nature of the decision and economic interest. (Regulation
18702.) Generally, where an economic interest is directly
involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.1 presumes that the
effect will be material.³ Thus, with respect to the recycling
contract, you may not participate.

Where an economic interest is not directly involved in a
decision, the official must still determine if the indirect effect
of the decision will be material. For example, Regulation 18702.5
provides materiality standards for nonprofit entities that may be
indirectly affected by a decision.

You stated that decisions concerning the collection of
garbage and the recycling issues have been segregated. Generally,
each governmental decision must be analyzed independently to
determine if there will be a foreseeable material financial effect
on an official's economic interests. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC
Ops. 77.) This would not be the case where two decisions are too
interrelated to be considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) This does not appear to be the case under your
facts.

You are not financially interested in the business entity
that currently contracts with the city to collect garbage.
Moreover, decisions concerning garbage service within the
jurisdiction would not appear to have any foreseeable financial
effect on the Environmental Center. Consequently, so long as the
garbage collection decisions will not financially affect the
recycling contract or the Environmental Center, you may
participate in the decisions regarding garbage service.




2 An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198.)

3 An economic interest is directly involved in a decision when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation
18702.1(b).)

Our File No. A-92-243
Page 4


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa




4
Copies of Commission regulations and opinions may be obtained
from the county law library. Copies of advice letters referenced
in this letter may be obtained from the Commission at a cost of
10¢ per page.
